step.name: Step 4

mainEntity.name: What are the RoC fees for capital increase?

acceptedAnswer.text: RoC fees are based on the incremental authorised capital amount. Example: ₹5,000 for increase up to ₹1 lakh, scaling to ₹25 lakh+ for capital above ₹50 crore. Stamp duty is additional and varies by state.

mainEntity.name: How to increase authorised capital of a company?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pass ordinary resolution at general meeting (simple majority). File SH-7 with RoC within 30 days. Pay additional stamp duty and filing fee. Amend MOA Clause V. No RoC approval needed — only filing.

mainEntity.name: What is the ROC fee for increasing authorised capital?

acceptedAnswer.text: Fee depends on increase amount. Up to ₹1 lakh: ₹2,000. ₹1-5 lakhs: ₹3,000. ₹5-10 lakhs: ₹4,000. ₹10-50 lakhs: ₹5,000. Above ₹50 lakhs: ₹5,000 + ₹500 per additional ₹1 lakh. Plus stamp duty (varies by state).

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between authorised and paid-up capital?

acceptedAnswer.text: Authorised capital: maximum amount of share capital a company can issue (set in MOA). Paid-up capital: amount actually paid by shareholders. Paid-up can never exceed authorised. Increase authorised first to issue more shares.

mainEntity.name: Is increase in authorised capital needed before raising funding?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, if new shares to be allotted would take total issued capital beyond current authorised capital. Must complete SH-7 filing before allotment. Otherwise, allotment is void under Section 39.
Yes, by passing special resolution and filing SH-7 for reduction. However, authorised capital cannot go below current paid-up capital. No stamp duty refund on reduction.
